description: Gravity argument enhancements: simple first-principles explanation + high/low/high pattern argument

patches.find: The Antarctic gravity low has multiple well-characterized standard-physics causes: (1) crustal thickness variations and a sub-lithospheric mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau producing a broad negative anomaly
patches.replace:
Why gravity varies only 0.53% on a globe (first principles): Two effects, both computable from a rotating oblate sphere, account for nearly all the variation. First, the equatorial bulge places the equator ~21 km farther from Earth's center of mass than the poles — by the inverse-square law, this reduces gravitational acceleration by ~0.2%. Second, centrifugal acceleration from Earth's rotation opposes gravity at the equator (~0.3% of g) but vanishes at the poles. Together: 0.2% + 0.3% ≈ 0.5%, matching the observed 0.53% to within measurement uncertainty. The WGS84 gravity formula the dome borrows encodes exactly these two effects — it is not an empirical mystery but a two-line derivation from oblate-sphere geometry.

The pattern problem — worse than the magnitude: On a globe, gravity follows a symmetric high/low/high pattern: ~9.832 m/s² at the north pole, dropping to ~9.780 m/s² at the equator, then recovering to ~9.832 m/s² at the south pole. The two poles have nearly equal gravity, with a minimum at the equator. On the dome, H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) decreases monotonically from the north pole (H = 8,537 km) through the equator (H ≈ 1,270 km) to the Antarctic rim (H ≈ 837 km). Under any H(r)-dependent mechanism, the predicted pattern is high (north pole) → medium (equator) → even lower (rim). Monotonic decline. No recovery. The observed gravity rising again south of the equator to match the north pole is impossible under any monotonically decreasing aetheric column height. The dome predicts high/medium/low; reality shows high/low/high. This is not just a 170× magnitude error — it is a qualitative shape-of-the-curve failure. The near-symmetry between the poles is the fingerprint of a sphere, not a disc with an exponential ceiling.

The residual Antarctic gravity low — the small departures from the simple oblate-sphere prediction — has multiple well-characterized standard-physics causes: (1) crustal thickness variations and a sub-lithospheric mantle plume beneath the East Antarctic Plateau producing a broad negative anomaly
patches.rationale: Add simple two-cause explanation (oblate bulge + centrifugal) and high/low/high pattern argument before complex geophysical causes

patches.find: The problem: The dome's exponential gravity formula predicts a dramatic drop: at r = 6,000 km (roughly South Africa), gravity has already fallen by 50%. At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), g drops to roughly 10% of its polar value — a 90% reduction. This is not a minor discrepancy. Measurements show gravity varies by only 0.53% across the entire Earth's surface, from ~9.78 m/s² at the equator to ~9.83 m/s² at the poles. The dome's geometry predicts a variation 170 times larger than observed, in the wrong direction (decreasing southward rather than increasing).
patches.replace:
The magnitude problem: The dome's exponential gravity formula predicts a dramatic drop: at r = 6,000 km (roughly South Africa), gravity has already fallen by 50%. At the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), g drops to roughly 10% of its polar value — a 90% reduction. Measurements show gravity varies by only 0.53% across the entire Earth's surface, from ~9.780 m/s² at the equator to ~9.832 m/s² at the poles. Why 0.53%? On an oblate rotating sphere, two first-principles effects account for nearly all of it: the equatorial bulge places the equator ~21 km farther from the center of mass (~0.2% less gravity), and centrifugal acceleration from rotation subtracts another ~0.3% at the equator. The dome's geometry predicts a variation 170 times larger than observed.

The pattern problem (qualitative failure): Even setting aside the 170× magnitude error, the shape of the gravity curve is wrong. Observed gravity follows a symmetric high/low/high pattern: ~9.832 m/s² at the north pole → ~9.780 m/s² at the equator → ~9.832 m/s² at the south pole. On the dome, H(r) decreases monotonically from north pole to rim, so any H(r)-dependent mechanism predicts high → medium → even lower. The gravity recovery at the south pole — rising back to match the north pole — is impossible under a monotonically decreasing dome height. The dome predicts high/medium/low; reality shows high/low/high. The near-symmetry between the two poles is the fingerprint of a sphere, not a disc with an exponential ceiling.
patches.rationale: Split problem into magnitude + pattern. Add first-principles explanation and high/low/high argument.

patches.find: The problem: The author's exponential gravity profile predicts gravity should decrease as you move south (increasing r). The formula g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r / 8,619) gives a 50% gravity drop by r = 6,000 km (somewhere in South Africa). Measurements show gravity varies smoothly by only 0.5% from pole to equator, with no such cliff. The author's fitted curve predicts a non-existent 90% gravity variation; measurements show 0.5%. The author resolves this by using the globe formula g = 9.7803 − 0.0325 × cos(2φ) − 0.0006 × cos²(2φ), which is derived from an oblate rotating sphere, not from his dome geometry.
patches.replace: The problem: The author's exponential gravity profile predicts gravity should decrease monotonically as you move south (increasing r). The formula g(r) = g₀ × exp(−r / 8,619) gives a 50% gravity drop by r = 6,000 km (somewhere in South Africa) and a 90% drop at the ice wall. Reality shows only 0.53% variation from equator to pole. But the problem goes deeper than magnitude: the shape of the observed curve contradicts the dome. Measured gravity goes high (north pole, ~9.832 m/s²) → low (equator, ~9.780 m/s²) → high again (south pole, ~9.832 m/s²). The near-equal values at both poles, with a minimum at the equator, are the signature of a symmetric rotating body. The dome's monotonically decreasing H(r) can only produce high → medium → low — it cannot produce a gravity minimum at the equator with recovery at the antipode. This is a qualitative shape failure, not merely a quantitative one. The author resolves it by abandoning the dome formula entirely and using the globe's WGS84 formula g = 9.7803 − 0.0325 × cos(2φ) − 0.0006 × cos²(2φ), which is derived from an oblate rotating sphere encoding the equatorial bulge (~0.2% effect) and centrifugal acceleration (~0.3% effect). This formula inherently produces the high/low/high pattern because it is symmetric about the equator — a property that cannot arise from flat-disc geometry with monotonic dome height.
patches.rationale: Expand from magnitude to shape-of-curve failure. Explain WGS84 high/low/high pattern and why dome cannot produce it.
